section.title: David and Bathsheba
section.sectionRationale: This is the tragic hinge of David's life and of the book. While his army fights at Rabbah, David lingers in Jerusalem, sees Bathsheba, takes her, and when she conceives, tries to cover it, first by manipulation and then by arranging the death of her husband Uriah. The chapter ends with the flat, damning verdict that the thing David had done was evil in the sight of the LORD. It is told with unsparing honesty as description, not endorsement, and it sets in motion the long unraveling of David's house.
section.thesis: At the height of his power David abuses it, taking another man's wife and then engineering the murder of a loyal man to hide his sin, and though he silences everyone else, he cannot silence the LORD, in whose sight the whole thing is simply evil, so that the sin of God's chosen king is exposed as sin and the consequences begin to gather.
section.frameNote: Three beats: David stays home, sees, and takes Bathsheba, and she conceives, verses 1 to 5; David's failed attempts to make Uriah cover the pregnancy, verses 6 to 13; and the arranged murder of Uriah, David's marriage to Bathsheba, and the LORD's verdict, verses 14 to 27.

units.label: David takes Bathsheba
units.note: he saw, he sent, he took

verses.text: In the spring, at the time when kings march out to war, David sent out Joab and his servants with the whole army of Israel. They destroyed the Ammonites and besieged Rabbah, but David remained in Jerusalem.
One evening David got up from his bed and strolled around on the roof of the palace. And from the roof he {{raah|saw}} a woman bathing, a very beautiful woman.
So David sent and inquired about the woman, and he was told, “This is Bathsheba, the daughter of Eliam and the wife of Uriah the Hittite.”
Then David {{shalach|sent}} messengers to get her, and when she came to him, he slept with her. (Now she had just purified herself from her uncleanness.) Then she returned home.
And the woman conceived and sent word to David, saying, “I am pregnant.”

units.label: David tries to cover his sin

verses.text: At this, David sent orders to Joab: “Send me Uriah the Hittite.” So Joab sent him to David.
When Uriah came to him, David asked how Joab and the troops were doing and how the war was going.
Then he said to Uriah, “Go down to your house and wash your feet.” So Uriah left the palace, and a gift from the king followed him.
But Uriah slept at the door of the palace with all his master’s servants; he did not go down to his house.
And David was told, “Uriah did not go home.” “Haven’t you just arrived from a journey?” David asked Uriah. “Why didn’t you go home?”

Uriah answered, “The ark and Israel and Judah are dwelling in tents, and my master Joab and his soldiers are camped in the open field. How can I go to my house to eat and drink and sleep with my wife? As surely as you live, and as your soul lives, I will not do such a thing!”
“Stay here one more day,” David said to Uriah, “and tomorrow I will send you back.” So Uriah stayed in Jerusalem that day and the next.
Then David invited Uriah to eat and drink with him, and he got Uriah drunk. And in the evening Uriah went out to lie down on his cot with his master’s servants, but he did not go home.

units.label: The murder of Uriah
units.note: evil in the sight of the LORD

verses.text: The next morning David wrote a letter to Joab and sent it with Uriah.
In the letter he wrote: “Put Uriah at the front of the fiercest battle; then withdraw from him, so that he may be struck down and killed.”
So as Joab besieged the city, he assigned Uriah to a place where he knew the strongest enemy soldiers were.
And when the men of the city came out and fought against Joab, some of David’s servants fell, and Uriah the Hittite also died.
Joab sent to David a full account of the battle
and instructed the messenger, “When you have finished giving the king a full account of the battle,
if the king’s anger flares, he may ask you, ‘Why did you get so close to the city to fight? Did you not realize they would shoot from atop the wall?

Who struck Abimelech son of Jerubbesheth? Was it not a woman who dropped an upper millstone on him from the wall, so that he died in Thebez? Why did you get so close to the wall?’ If he asks you this, then you are to say, ‘Your servant Uriah the Hittite is dead as well.’”
So the messenger set out and reported to David all that Joab had sent him to say.
The messenger said to David, “The men overpowered us and came out against us in the field, but we drove them back to the entrance of the gate.
Then the archers shot at your servants from the wall, and some of the king’s servants were killed. And your servant Uriah the Hittite is dead as well.”

Then David told the messenger, “Say this to Joab: ‘Do not let this matter upset you, for the sword devours one as well as another. Strengthen your attack against the city and demolish it.’ Encourage him with these words.”
When Uriah’s wife heard that her husband was dead, she mourned for him.
And when the time of mourning was over, David had her brought to his house, and she became his wife and bore him a son. But the thing that David had done was {{ra|evil}} in the sight of the LORD.

shalach.gloss: To send. The verb drums through the chapter as the mark of David's misused royal power: David sent Joab to war, sent to inquire, sent messengers to take Bathsheba, sent for Uriah, sent the deadly letter. The one who should have gone out to battle instead stays home and 'sends,' commanding others to serve his sin, until the LORD sends Nathan to confront him in the next chapter.

ra.gloss: Evil, wickedness, that which is bad and displeasing. The chapter's final, unadorned verdict is that the thing David had done was ra, evil, in the sight of the LORD. However cleverly David silenced Uriah, Joab, and the court, the narrator lets God's single word stand over it all: not a mistake, not a misfortune, but evil in the eyes of the God who sees.

raah.gloss: To see, look at, perceive. David 'saw' a beautiful woman from the roof, and the chain of sin follows from that look pursued rather than turned away. The same root stands behind the verdict that David's deed was evil in the 'sight,' the seeing, of the LORD, so that the God who truly sees answers the king's fateful looking.

basic.context: This is the darkest chapter in David's story and the turning point of the book. It is spring, the season when kings lead their armies, but David sends Joab and the army to besiege the Ammonite city of Rabbah while he stays behind in Jerusalem. Restless one evening, he sees from his roof a beautiful woman bathing. He learns she is Bathsheba, wife of Uriah the Hittite, one of his own soldiers. Knowing she is married, David sends for her and sleeps with her, and she conceives. To cover it up, David brings Uriah home from the front, hoping he will sleep with his wife and appear to be the child's father. But Uriah, more honorable than his king, refuses to enjoy home comforts while the ark and the army are in the field, even when David gets him drunk. So David sends Uriah back carrying his own death warrant: a letter ordering Joab to place him where he will be killed. Joab complies, Uriah dies, and David marries the widow. The chapter closes with the stark verdict that stands over everything: the thing David had done was evil in the sight of the LORD. It raises the questions of how a man after God's own heart falls so far, how one sin breeds another, and who finally sees and judges.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative told with unflinching honesty. It is description, not prescription: the Bible does not excuse or endorse David's adultery and murder but exposes them, and the closing verdict makes the moral judgment explicit. The account resists blaming Bathsheba; the king holds the power, and the verbs of taking and sending are all David's. The chapter should not be moralized into a simple lesson about idle glances only, but read as a sober revelation that even God's chosen and greatly blessed king is a sinner who needs grace, and that sin, when hidden, compounds into deeper evil.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is built on a chain of active verbs that all belong to David: he saw, he sent, he took, he lay, he sent again. The verb shalach, to send, recurs like a drumbeat, the king who should have marched out to battle instead stays home and sends others to serve his sin. It all begins with David's seeing, raah, a look pursued rather than turned from. And it ends with the single Hebrew word ra, evil: the thing David had done was evil in the sight, the seeing, of the LORD. The God who truly sees answers the king's fateful looking with a verdict no cover-up can escape.
basic.typology: David here is the very opposite of the king Israel needs, abusing his power to take and to kill, an anti-type that makes the world long for a King who will use His power to give and to save rather than to grasp and destroy. Uriah, the loyal, righteous man betrayed and sent to his death by the one he served, faintly foreshadows the truly righteous One betrayed to death, though here the guilt is entirely the king's. The chapter's exposure of the best king's sin drives the story toward the sinless King who alone is worthy.
basic.christ: David's fall shows that even the greatest son of Israel is a sinner who cannot save himself or his people, deepening the need for a King who will never abuse His power. Jesus is that King: tempted in every way yet without sin, He used His power not to take a bride by force but to give His life for His bride, and not to send a faithful man to death but to go to death Himself for the unfaithful. Where David sent Uriah to die to cover his own sin, Christ went to die to cover the sins of others, the exact reverse, and the only remedy for a sin like David's.
basic.god: God is the one whose sight frames the whole chapter: however David silences every human witness, the LORD sees, and His verdict, 'evil,' stands unanswerable. God is holy and just, unwilling to wink at sin even in His chosen king, and yet the larger story will show Him to be merciful, sending a prophet to bring David to repentance rather than simply to destruction. The chapter reveals a God who is not mocked, from whom no cover-up is hidden, and who will deal truthfully with the sin of those He loves.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 51 is David's later prayer of repentance over this very sin. Second Samuel 12 records Nathan's confrontation and the consequences. James 1 traces how desire, when it conceives, gives birth to sin and death, the pattern seen here. And Hebrews 4 presents Christ as the King tempted in every way yet without sin.
basic.oneStory: At the summit of his success, God's chosen king falls into adultery and murder, and the narrator lets God's verdict, 'evil in the sight of the LORD,' stand over it all. This tragic hinge exposes the deep flaw in even the best human king and turns the whole story toward its need for a sinless King, Jesus, who would use His power not to take life but to lay down His own, the only answer to sin like David's.

context.literary: The chapter is the pivot of 2 Samuel, dividing David's rise from his long decline. It opens with pointed irony, 'when kings march out to war, David remained in Jerusalem,' and is driven by a relentless chain of verbs of taking and sending, all David's. The honorable Uriah is set as a foil to the scheming king. The single sentence of verse 27, 'the thing that David had done was evil in the sight of the LORD,' lands like a hammer, the narrator's and God's judgment on the whole episode.
context.historical: Spring was campaign season, so David's staying home already signals a king shirking his duty. A palace roof overlooked lower houses. Uriah the Hittite, a foreigner, was among David's elite warriors, and his refusal to go home reflects the practice of ritual and military consecration during holy war. 'Washing the feet' was a euphemism for going home to one's wife. Sending a condemning letter by the victim's own hand shows both David's ruthlessness and the trust Uriah placed in him.
context.canonical: This chapter is the background of Psalm 51, one of the great penitential psalms, and its consequences shape the rest of 2 Samuel, fulfilling the covenant warning that David's sons would be disciplined. The New Testament still names David in the Messiah's genealogy 'by the wife of Uriah,' Matthew 1:6, refusing to hide the scandal, and holds up the sinlessness of Jesus against the fallenness of even the greatest king, deepening the Bible's hope for a Redeemer.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record honestly the grievous sin of God's chosen king, adultery compounded by deceit and murder, and to let God's own verdict of 'evil' stand over it, so that no reader mistakes David's greatness for sinlessness and all are pointed to the need for grace and for a King who will not fall.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter is pure description of sin and its cover-up, never prescription; the Bible neither excuses nor commends David's actions but exposes and condemns them in verse 27. What it teaches is the reality that even the godliest can fall grievously, that hidden sin breeds further sin, that misused power crushes the vulnerable, and that God sees and judges what people try to conceal, all of which drives the reader to repentance and to Christ.

debates.question: What is Bathsheba's role, and does the text place any blame on her?

views.label: The narrative frames David as the guilty aggressor
views.case: Every decisive verb belongs to David, he saw, sent, took, and lay, and the power of a king summoning a subject's wife is coercive; the text places the moral weight squarely on David, whom Nathan will indict as the rich man who stole the poor man's lamb, chapter 12, and Scripture consistently calls it David's sin, never Bathsheba's, so she is best understood as the wronged party, a woman with little power to refuse the king.

views.label: A tragedy in which David bears the overwhelming guilt
views.case: Some observe that the text says little of Bathsheba's inner response and does not narrate resistance, so it stops short of a full account of her role; yet even on this reading the chapter's whole thrust, and Nathan's parable, lay the guilt on David's abuse of power, so any ambiguity about Bathsheba does nothing to lessen the king's culpability.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that David is the guilty party the text indicts; the chapter is a study in the abuse of royal power against the vulnerable, and its verdict, 'evil in the sight of the LORD,' falls on David, not on Bathsheba or Uriah, guarding us against blaming the victim of a powerful man's sin.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is constructed from a chain of active verbs that all belong to David: he saw, he sent, he took, he lay with her, he sent again. The verb shalach, to send, beats through the passage like a war drum, and it exposes the irony of the opening: it is 'the time when kings march out to war,' yet David does not march, he stays home and sends, commanding Joab, messengers, Bathsheba, and finally Uriah, to serve his sin, even carrying his own death warrant. The whole descent begins with a single act of seeing, raah, a look lingered over rather than turned away. And it closes with one blunt Hebrew word, ra, evil: 'the thing that David had done was evil in the sight of the LORD,' verse 27. The God who genuinely sees answers the king's fateful looking; every human witness has been silenced or killed, but the seeing God renders a verdict that no scheme can overturn, and that verdict, evil, hangs over the whole glittering cover-up.

typology.type: The king who abuses his power to take and to kill
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the King who used His power not to take but to give His life for His bride, Mark 10:45 and Ephesians 5:25, the very opposite of David here.

typology.type: Uriah, the loyal righteous man betrayed to death by the one he served
typology.fulfillment: A faint, inverted foreshadowing of the righteous One betrayed to death, though here the guilt lies wholly with the king, pointing to the need for a sinless King, Hebrews 4:15.

typology.type: The best human king exposed as a sinner
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the sinless King who alone is worthy and can save sinners like David, Hebrews 7:26.

study.christ: David's fall shows that even the greatest son of Israel is a sinner who cannot save himself or his people, and it deepens the ache for a King who will never abuse His power. Jesus is that King: tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin, Hebrews 4:15. Where David used his power to take another man's wife, Christ used His power to give His life and win His bride, Ephesians 5:25. Where David sent a faithful man to die in order to hide his own sin, Christ went to death Himself to bear the sins of the unfaithful, the exact reverse of David's crime and the only remedy for it. Astonishingly, the gospel does not erase this scandal but redeems it: Matthew's genealogy names Jesus as descended from David 'by the wife of Uriah,' Matthew 1:6, so that grace runs even through David's darkest sin to the sinless Savior.
study.god: God is the one whose sight frames the entire chapter: however completely David silences Uriah, manipulates Joab, and manages the court, the LORD sees, and His one-word verdict, 'evil,' stands unanswerable over all of it. God is holy and just, refusing to wink at sin even in the king He had so blessed and called a man after His own heart. Yet the very fact that the story continues, that God will send Nathan to bring David to repentance rather than simply strike him down, reveals a God who is merciful as well as just, who deals truthfully but redemptively with the sins of those He loves. He is not mocked, and no cover-up is hidden from Him.

crossReferences.note: David's prayer of repentance over this sin, confessing 'against You, You only, have I sinned and done what is evil in Your sight.'
Nathan's parable and confrontation, God's response to the evil of this chapter, bringing David to repentance and announcing consequences.
Desire, when it has conceived, gives birth to sin, and sin brings forth death, the very pattern of David's descent.
The genealogy of Jesus names David as father of Solomon 'by the wife of Uriah,' refusing to hide the scandal and showing grace running through it to Christ.

study.oneStory: At the very summit of his success, God's chosen king plunges into adultery and murder, and the narrator lets God's verdict, 'evil in the sight of the LORD,' stand over the whole episode. This tragic hinge lays bare the deep flaw in even the best human king and turns the story decisively toward its need for a sinless King. That King is Jesus, the Son of David who would not grasp but give, who used His power not to take life but to lay down His own, and through whom grace reaches even to a sin as dark as this, so that the scandal of David becomes, in the genealogy of the Messiah, a testimony to redeeming grace.

quiz.q: What is the significance of the note that David remained in Jerusalem while his army went to war?

options.text: It is a pointed irony: it was the season when kings led their armies, yet David stayed home, and it is there, idle, that his sin begins.
options.note: Verse 1 sets up the failure by noting David stayed home at the very time kings march out to war.

options.text: It simply explains that David was too busy governing to fight.
options.note: The text gives no such justification; it highlights the irony that the king was not where kings should be.

options.text: It shows David neglecting his royal duty, which opens the door to temptation.
options.note: True, but this option softens the sharp irony that it was precisely the time when kings go out to war.

options.text: It proves David had already grown lazy and cowardly in battle.
options.note: David was a proven warrior; the point is his being out of place this once, not a settled cowardice.

quiz.q: How does one sin lead David deeper in this chapter?

options.text: Adultery leads to a cover-up, and when that fails, to arranging the murder of the loyal Uriah.
options.note: Verses 6-17 show David's sin compounding from adultery to deceit to murder.

options.text: David confesses his sin openly as soon as Bathsheba conceives.
options.note: The opposite; David tries to hide the sin rather than confessing it.

options.text: David tries to hide his sin and it escalates to further wrongdoing.
options.note: True, but this option omits the specific, chilling escalation to arranging Uriah's death by his own hand.

options.text: David repents privately but continues to struggle with temptation.
options.note: There is no repentance in this chapter; that comes only after Nathan's confrontation in chapter 12.

quiz.q: How is Uriah portrayed in contrast to David?

options.text: As an honorable, loyal soldier who refuses home comforts while the ark and army are in the field, even when David gets him drunk.
options.note: Verses 9-13 show Uriah's integrity standing in sharp contrast to the king's scheming.

options.text: As a foolish man who fails to see through David's plan.
options.note: Uriah is portrayed as honorable, not foolish; his integrity, not naivety, is the point.

options.text: As a faithful man whose devotion exposes David's failure by contrast.
options.note: True, but this option omits the vivid details of his refusal to go home even when made drunk.

options.text: As a coward who hid at the palace door to avoid returning to battle.
options.note: Uriah slept at the palace door out of solidarity with the army, not cowardice; he later returns to the front and dies.

quiz.q: What is the chapter's final verdict on what David has done?

options.text: That the thing David had done was evil in the sight of the LORD.
options.note: Verse 27 lets God's one-word judgment, evil, stand over the whole episode.

options.text: That David had made an understandable mistake under pressure.
options.note: The text calls it evil, not an understandable mistake; it offers no excuse.

options.text: That David's actions displeased God and were wicked in His eyes.
options.note: True, but this option loses the stark, unadorned force of the single verdict 'evil in the sight of the LORD.'

options.text: That the matter was regrettable but ultimately a private affair.
options.note: The chapter insists it was evil before God, not a merely private or regrettable affair.

quiz.q: How does this account of David's sin point us to Christ?

options.text: It exposes even the best king as a sinner, deepening the need for the sinless King who uses His power to give His life, not to take another's.
options.note: David's fall drives the story toward Christ, the King without sin, Hebrews 4:15 and Ephesians 5:25.

options.text: It teaches that great leaders are entitled to bend the rules for the greater good.
options.note: The chapter condemns David's abuse of power as evil; it grants no such entitlement.

options.text: It shows the need for a King who will not abuse His power over the vulnerable.
options.note: True, but this option omits the striking contrast with Christ who gives His life rather than taking, and even redeems David's line.

options.text: It proves that God's promises to David were withdrawn because of his sin.
options.note: God disciplines but does not withdraw the covenant; grace even runs through this sin to Christ in the genealogy.

quiz.q: Where does the text place the guilt for what happened?

options.text: Squarely on David, whose power and choices drive every step, and whom God and Nathan indict.
options.note: The verbs are all David's, and chapter 12 names him the guilty party, guarding against blaming the victim.

options.text: On David, though the narrative leaves some questions about others' roles.
options.note: True that the text is spare about Bathsheba, but this option understates how decisively the guilt is laid on David.

options.text: Equally on David and Bathsheba as partners in sin.
options.note: The text and Nathan's parable lay the moral weight on David's abuse of royal power, not equally on Bathsheba.

options.text: Mainly on Joab for carrying out the order.
options.note: Joab is complicit, but the chapter and God's verdict place the guilt on David, whose scheme it was.

questions: David's fall began when he was idle and out of place, not where he was meant to be. Where in your life are you most vulnerable when you drift from your calling or responsibilities?
David saw, and then he sent; the look became a choice. How do you handle the difference between a temptation that comes and a temptation you pursue?
One hidden sin drove David to deceive and finally to kill to protect himself. Where might you be tempted to cover a sin rather than confess it, and where could that road lead?
David silenced every human witness, but he could not silence the LORD who sees. How does living before the God who sees everything change the way you act when no one is watching?

The gospel runs grace even through David's darkest sin, naming Jesus as descended from 'the wife of Uriah.' How does that assure you that no failure puts you beyond God's redeeming reach when you repent?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes that 'in the spring' is literally 'at the turn of the year,' the season when kings led their armies to war, which sharpens the irony of David's staying in Jerusalem.
David's instruction that Uriah 'wash your feet' and go down to his house is a euphemism for going home to be with his wife, part of David's scheme to make Uriah appear the child's father.

The closing sentence, 'the thing that David had done was evil in the sight of the LORD,' is the narrator's and God's explicit moral verdict, framing the whole chapter as description of sin, not endorsement; David later echoes it in Psalm 51:4.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Samuel 11:1-27. An em dash in the source at verse 2 was converted to a comma; no dashes remain in this file. David's sin is handled as honest description, not prescription, with the guilt placed on David as the text does.
